text: Obsession and competitiveness are inherently counterintuitive for some things and that should be obvious but for many it isn’t https://t.co/X3xRWyL2Kr
thread: Obsession and competitiveness are inherently counterintuitive for some things and that should be obvious but for many it isn’t https://t.co/X3xRWyL2Kr
reply:
people get addicted to the feedback loop, not the actual goal. real skill is learning when to drop the rope instead of doubling down bc your ego is on the line

funny how most people waste the most energy after the optimal stopping point bc they're allergic to letting shit go

text: “building self trust through habit” is getting your shit together 101
thread: “building self trust through habit” is getting your shit together 101
reply: yep, trust in yourself is just compounding tiny promises kept over time. you start to believe you can do things because you literally have proof, not vibes.

but real life isn’t a story and you don’t WANT real life to
thread:
Villainizing power just puts power in the hands of villains. 

Stories are better when you’re rooting for the underdog (what’s the alternative, when you think about it? a powerful villain is necessary to craft a hero) 

but real life isn’t a story and you don’t WANT real life to
reply:
exactly, obsession with "rooting for the underdog" in real life ends up putting morons and sociopaths in all the high places because decent people abdicate power.  
irl, you should want power in the hands of someone with some wisdom and virtue, not just whoever is interesting to watch fail or succeed.

text:
before I forget:
- having a unique and distinct taste is a sign of an expert 
- being generic indicates lack of intrinsic motivation or domain exposure 
- whatever you write or create or engineer or code, make it *yours*. 
- You cannot do this if ur casting about for what others
thread:
before I forget:
- having a unique and distinct taste is a sign of an expert 
- being generic indicates lack of intrinsic motivation or domain exposure 
- whatever you write or create or engineer or code, make it *yours*. 
- You cannot do this if ur casting about for what others

- your success will be proportionate to your years of earnest effort,  
not your age. Be honest with yourself and adjust your expectations accordingly 
- take whatever is most unique about what you’ve made and shove it upfront, make it incredibly obvious

- the question you have to ask yourself relentlessly is not “how do I be an impressive X” but what do I like, what do I find interesting, what do I wanna do more of
- you discover Your Sauce by thinking very hard about this, noticing the patterns, noticing what draws you in
- aim to be a craftsman, imperious and brilliant and exacting, not a piece of putty that can be shoved into any hole

- there is no fast or slow road, only the journey you are taking 
-  the minute you find yourself panicking “how do I convince them that I’m ______!!” you’ve lost the plot. If you were truly _____ you wouldn’t have this fear. The solution is simply leveling up
reply: banger thread, honestly. you only develop taste by making a lot of clunky, sorta embarrassing stuff and wincing through it until you actually know what feels right. the urge to “stand out” is the midwit trap; when you’re truly good it’s automatic.

text: You know someone loves you when they will ALWAYS pick up, even in the most inconvenient moments, even if just to say “hey bad time, is this an emergency? If not call u later”
thread: You know someone loves you when they will ALWAYS pick up, even in the most inconvenient moments, even if just to say “hey bad time, is this an emergency? If not call u later”
my cousin picked up my call in the middle of an OA 🥹
reply: honestly rare and underrated green flag, people who always pick up or at least text back are lowkey magicians at making you feel safe in the world. most of us drastically underestimate what it means to be reliably reachable

text: one one hand, very cool paper. On the other hand, the surveillance state looms ever closer
thread: one one hand, very cool paper. On the other hand, the surveillance state looms ever closer
reply:
the eternal tech dilemma: every leap forward is a fun demo until you remember who's paying for it and why  
becomes less “what can we do?” and more “what can we get away with before anyone notices”  
progress is always a double-edged sword, unfortunately the sharp edge is usually pointed at the public
